Hopefully not to Hollywood. —Anthony Breznican There isn’t a lot of evidence of the distant past left in San Francisco, which has famously been wracked by earthquakes and, more importantly, did something quite strange in 1900—outsource eternity. The Board of Supervisors forbade burials in the geographically constrained City by the Bay and eventually exhumed some 125,000 corpses to make space. They were reburied in nearby Colma, a vast necropolis to the south. You can still buy T-shirts there reading, “It’s great to be alive in Colma.” But Mission Dolores still has its cemetery, and on this afternoon, mission curator Andrew Galvan is leading a tour. In tow are three dozen docents from missions up and down California, visiting for the 43rd Annual California Missions and Presidios Conference. Mission Dolores: a Catholic church of Spanish-colonial provenance that celebrated its first mass five days before the Declaration of Independence was signed. For Californians of a certain age, creating sugar-cube dioramas of missions was a de rigueur elementary school project. “We try to discourage that today,” says Galvan, the descendant of Ohlone Indians who were rounded up and taken here against their will. “We don’t have students make dioramas of concentration camps when they’re studying World War II or plantations when they’re discussing slavery. We’re really trying to dispel the romantic story of California’s missions and tell the true story.” The curator’s great-great-great-great-grandparents, Faustino and Obulinda, lie below, along with almost fourteen thousand others. The cemetery used to be significantly larger than its current small fraction of a city block: Mission Dolores, like San Francisco, repurposed its burial grounds. “If you parked in our schoolyard,” Galvan says with a nod toward distant ballplayers shooting hoops, “you probably parked on top of somebody.” Everybody gasps. And then they laugh. It’s a bit surprising in San Francisco, where future generations can get just as little deference as those past. A block away at Dolores Park, like the mission named for a buried creek, a quick count of the north section reveals twenty-one dogs and just one child. The city has long had the lowest percentage of children in not just the United States but all of North America. In the park a man attempts a handstand on a yoga mat while four young men in tank tops play spikeball; Johnny Griffin’s 1956 rendition of “These Foolish Things” wafts out of their Bluetooth speaker. Dolores Park, too, was once a cemetery. But on this Friday, the past, like the future, is out of sight and out of mind. It is a serene spring evening, and all are enjoying the glorious present. —Joe Eskenazi By April most of the cruise ships have left San Juan Harbor for the Mediterranean, even though the weather here remains pleasant. So on a day like this, after coffee shops close and the remaining tourists embark, the streets of El Viejo San Juan belong once again to the locals—locals like Angie and Alfonso, a couple in their late sixties—and to the memories that linger here. Today it is not too hot, not too windy. Angie’s hat did not fly away. This often happens with the winds around Castillo San Felipe del Morro, where Angie and Alfonso started their afternoon walk, a walk they take often. The old city—the oldest under U.S. jurisdiction, as Alfonso clarifies—is clean and well maintained, they note. Angie is notoriously the first to comment about trash on sidewalks or when places look in decay. She’s political in that way and would quickly post about it on Facebook, whereas Alfonso tends to have a nostalgic allegiance. But both would agree that the narrow streets and architecture are a “treasure gifted from the past.” It sounds more poetic in Spanish. They pass by La Iglesía San José and sit in the plaza that overlooks it. Alfonso, a history buff who excels at occasionally useless trivia, recounts how the remains of Juan Ponce de León, Puerto Rico’s first governor, who later named Florida, were there before being moved to La Catedral down the street. In churches Angie simply finds solemnity. This one is the second oldest in the Americas. They got married here in 1982; they will celebrate their forty-fourth anniversary this June. These streets, or adoquines, far predate their marriage and their lives. Even in colonial times they were known for their festivals and for witnessing miracles. Angie’s grandmother, a San Juanera, was born here and loved sharing ghost stories. Angie’s favorite concerned a jockey somehow surviving his horse’s fall off a ledge after a race. It took place in La Calle del Cristo, which connects the church where she got married and La Catedral, where Juan Ponce de León is now buried and where Alfonso was baptized in 1956. He recalls waiting on its steps to take his son home from SanSe, the annual street festival that serves as a rite of passage for most Puerto Rican teenagers—aka their first drink of many. Alfonso chuckles, remembering how he escaped there when he was younger, hinting at his own debauchery. Rounding the corner, they turn onto Calle Fortaleza, home to the oldest executive mansion in continuous use in the U.S., near where Alfonso lived as a newborn—and where Angie feels connected to ancestors and a family no longer there. She points out an apartment where a cannonball hit her maternal great-grandparents’ balcony during the Spanish–American War in 1898. Thankfully there were no casualties, but it’s fair to say that the balcony no longer exists. Next they walk by Angie’s father’s father’s old storefront. He imported European furniture, hardware supplies that were hard to find at the time, and even German toys. Good toys, she says. Alfonso eagerly steers them down La Calle Tetuán, where his father owned a business when Old San Juan was the commercial capital of the island. They can’t help themselves, sharing fond memories across generations of places and tales of loved ones no longer here. And what of the future? Now they loudly debate, rather excitedly and too eagerly, about what time they’ll pick me up from the SJU airport when I return for the holidays, when Angie will decorate the house and wait to see what I notice, while Alfonso heads straight to the kitchen to prepare me an egg breakfast—a Spanish tortilla or rustic eggs in a cazuela. The son at SanSe, I should say, was me. Angie and Alfonso are my parents, and I am a loved one who is no longer here. I ask my parents to share these stories with me as it’s been sixteen years since I moved to the continental U.S. and left my precious island. The longer I’ve been away, the more I long for a spoken history unknown to any textbook. It can be lonely even in New York City, just a three-and-a-half-hour flight away. Many Americans question Puerto Ricans’ worth—even our Super Bowl halftime performance. I question the history classes that failed to teach them. Many insist on botching our language and misinterpreting our culture. My identity gets questioned; my passport too. But hearing my parents recount our rich history, one older than the U.S., a history that dates further back than 1776 but takes place in America, I am reminded that we are American, but maybe we’ll say it in Spanish: americano. —Alfonso Fernández Navas Everything broke for the better by evening. The rain had blasted through Harvey’s oyster farm in late afternoon, in drenching sheets and gusts that whipped against the dock and shoreline. It stung my face before ending as abruptly as it began. Bluff Hill Cove, one of the jewels of Point Judith Pond in Narragansett, Rhode Island, turned calm. I walked to the end of the dock, pulled a mossy line, and lifted a floating crate from the flowing tide. Oysters my son Mick had raised rattled inside. Winter had been brutally cold, so frigid that the pond’s saltwater surface froze solid for weeks. The oysters and clams beneath that snow-covered lid were dormant. But with the slowly warming temperatures of April, shellfish were waking up. I picked an oyster from Mick’s stash. The leading edge of its two shells, away from the hinge, showed a faint white crust resembling a fingernail – the telltale sign of fresh growth. A flash of satisfaction moved me. At last the natural new year was upon us. It was audible too, in the songs of a yellow-rumped warbler, a red-winged blackbird, an American robin, all migratory birds freshly returned, flitting through the sumac along the shore. Oyster aquaculture and wild-clam harvesting are demanding. But with their natural rhythms, their body-straining labors, and their succulent results, they serve as counterpoints and complements to the intensities that play out onshore. Here you are on nature’s calendar and clock, governed by weather, season, and tides, which together are a blend of the predictable and the seemingly random. We entered the game as a family when the kids were smaller, a way to access healthy local food and the work of the water together. Now it’s a business, helping Mick pay his medical school bills while employing other watermen and providing fresh raw oysters to markets that a generation of farmers before us—Harvey and Tom—opened with vision and hard work. Now there is work for our family: a used skiff motor to swap out for a less used replacement, oyster cages to haul, crop to inspect, new seed to put into the water for the seven months of growth before cold slows it all down again. Mick will lead. I will follow. Other sons will join. My phone vibrates in my pocket. Friday evening. I flinch a little. It could be any number of day-job bosses asking boss things. I took to the water in part to slip away from people, politics, priorities, and pain I do not pretend to understand. Here on Bluff Hill Cove Oyster Farm, or just up the channel at the North Star lease, life is better. Damn that phone. I retrieve it from my pocket to check. It’s the best boss of all: my father, in his eighty-eighth year. “Are the oysters growing again now?” he asks by text. I smile. We’re in rhythm, and maybe, just maybe, like the weather at this moment, everything will be okay. —Chris Chivers We got a call as soon as the evening shift started. There was a preemie baby at a hospital who needed to be transferred to the Neonatal Intensive Care Unit at the University of Michigan. The flight is quick, only about fifty minutes long, but it required a ton of preparation. A NICU call is different from what we do normally. A premature baby is so small they can’t regulate their body temperature, so we have to bring an Isolette, which is a device that helps monitor and regulate the baby’s temperature. We also have to load up special equipment like airway tools small enough to fit them, as well as medications. Everything goes onto the helicopter. It was a pretty packed crew: our pilot, me, another flight nurse, plus a NICU nurse, a NICU nurse practitioner, and a NICU respiratory therapist. Most of us had never met each other before. When we landed, the helipad wasn’t right next to the hospital, so we had to take an ambulance to pick up the baby. That meant transferring the Isolette to the ambulance. It’s heavy—it took six of us to move it. I mixed IV drips to connect to the machine, which would keep the baby alive during the flight. This kid was only around five or six pounds. It was tiny. When we got back to the helicopter, the security guy had to help us lift the Isolette back in. I began checking the inverter switch on the helicopter. We’re pessimists. It’s really important that power is running to fuel the Isolette and all the medical equipment. After takeoff, I started running through worst-case scenarios in my head, but the infant’s heart rate, temperature, and blood pressure were maintaining stability. When we were about twenty minutes out, I called in a report to our hospital and gave the dispatcher the infant’s main diagnosis and informed them of the IV drips and medications the infant was on so they could prepare for our arrival. We touched down just about midnight, and then we did the whole thing all over again to get the baby inside. The baby made it just fine. Usually by the time I show up, the family has exhausted all their other efforts. When they see me, they get fresh hope. The looks on the faces of the patient’s family are always unforgettable. —Katelyn Dykas, survival flight nurse with Michigan Medicine, as told to Sirena He It’s another night in a spring that’s coming to Boston piecemeal, one day at a time, only during the daylight hours. My daughter and I are at Fenway Park to see the Red Sox play the Detroit Tigers, two old-school franchises, currently very mediocre, that opened their ballparks the same week the Titanic went down. Detroit’s Tiger Stadium went down in 2009. Fenway remains. It’s gaudier than the first time I went, on a rainy afternoon in 1964. The Red Sox groaned toward an eighth-place finish in the as-yet-undivided American League. The park is still the best place to pass an evening, even with all the bells and whistles of modern “game presentation” crammed into it. I manage to survive the pop hits that play between each half inning, culminating in “Sweet Caroline” before the bottom of the eighth. I even manage to tolerate Boston’s green jerseys. The taut, wonderful game goes into the tenth inning scoreless. The chill deepens. Boston’s Jarren Duran gets on third base, and the Detroit infield draws in to cut him off at the plate. Pinch hitter Masataka Yoshida bounces a ball over the head of Tiger first baseman Spencer Torkelson for a game-winning single. The music cranks—Dropkick Murphys this time—and they blink the ballpark lights on and off a couple times. “Game presentation.” —Charles P. Pierce In my memory, our table was inside which it couldn’t have been. There are no dining tables inside only outside in the garden, hung with silk Fortuny lamps and insulated from the improbably close Sunset Strip by a dense cage of ivy. It’s French Gothic except you are drinking a margarita. To an Angeleno, the exoticism of a French combination bistro-­palace is consonant with the exoticism of a margarita. When my friend and her boyfriend eventually propose having a threesome with me, I am already on the brink of nausea from afternoon tamales, Mexican Japanese fusion tuna tartare, and margaritas. I lunge toward the bathroom nearly ruffling the velvet cape of Anya, the notorious doorkeeper of the hotel, as she applies her famous red lipstick. Sometimes you are the girl taking pictures in the mirror while another is throwing up, sometimes it’s the other way around. Fifteen minutes later I tell the couple I can’t stay. I do not go home but upstairs where I order a bottle of Champagne and two glasses which I take to the pool. No one stops me walking barefoot with an open bottle through the halls, or wearing only my cotton underwear and no bra in the water. In fact there is no one around at all. Lyric was the tallest of our friends and it would fall to her to stand on the bamboo chair at the entrance to the pool and reach over for the handle when we were teenagers. This was back when you could still get away with this. When there was parking on Sunset and my brother’s tae kwon do class shared a lot with the Googie bank across the street that has since been demolished. No one ever stopped us or questioned that we should be there, jettisoning the most stylish black outfits we could come up with across the damp bricks. If we wanted to appear as believable hotel guests we could have worn sweatpants and swimsuits, but this never occurred to our teenage minds. At sixteen I was often wearing a men’s black silk kimono with my mom’s borrowed couture slacks from the nineties. Lyric had her mom’s sparkly beaded Fendi purse. We would get bottles of St-Germain and sparkling water from Bogie’s Liquors in Hancock Park, where we went to school; I photoshopped a scan of my passport to say 1986 because I was the best at drawing. We made French 75’s with elderflower instead of gin because gin was gross. Looking back at the final ten years of Lyric’s life—from those nights skinny-dipping in the pool, to when I am vomiting from a broken heart and the night is still quiet and vacant, though they have removed the glass balls from the potted plants surrounding the pool, and replaced the dingy navy-and-white-striped lounge chairs with twice as many pristine pastel ones—we felt so old. Older than our parents could have possibly imagined we’d be by sixteen, having not anticipated the Internet. We knew everything. Our history, like California’s, like the hotel’s, is one after ourselves. The simulation becomes real with enough repetition, and now we really are thirty-year-old women, the tallest of us is dead, and this fantastical place to which our parents came to make movies with only their own made-up future before them has actually become our home, a place to which we return with nostalgia for a past both real and imagined. Tonight I’m sitting in the lobby, building paper airplanes with the owner’s son. On a date with an assistant producer, another man, an agent, sends me a scoop of chocolate gelato from across the room. Violet, Nathalie, and I are going to an astrology-themed birthday party later dressed as Virgo, Taurus, and Scorpio. “This a stealth bomber, and this one is for stunts.” We are throwing the paper airplanes toward the guy who is buying up empty lots to flip in the Palisades burn zone. It’s April now, everyone says there will be no more rain. —Mercedes Kilmer I’m a touch lightheaded but not enough to dull the electric guitar from the live music or the gnats buzzing in my ear. I hate bugs, but they are typical of Georgia’s warmer weather. The trade-off for being at a “darty”—University of Georgia shorthand for an outdoor, daytime frat party. There’s a tap on my shoulder. “Will you guys wait for me if I move in on this guy?” Hannah asks. I roll my eyes playfully. “Knock yourself out.” It’s Lambdapalooza at Lambda Chi Alpha, where every woman wears a floral dress and, unlike the guys, gets in for free. I am here with my friends Hannah, Simer, and Gianna, plus seven other girls. Most of them are friends of Gianna. I haven’t quite learned all their names, though I suppose I won’t have the chance to after tonight. Three weeks from today, my girlfriends and I will graduate. A Lambda brother approaches our group and pulls out a canister of mint-flavored Zyns. One of my girlfriends excitedly opens her mouth as the brother plops one onto her tongue. “Abort mission, abort mission!” Hannah bursts out of the crowd, returning to our group after recognizing an ex-lover. The Lambda invites us into the house, and we file inside. The bedroom we end up in is lined with dark-oak walls and Christmas lights. Shelves of emptied whiskey bottles. What I think is a deer skull adjacent to the leather couch my girlfriends and I sit on. “I have whiskey, rum, bourbon, and wine,” says the nameless Lambda Chi. “Any mixers?” one of the girls slurs. “I’ll take a glass of red, please,” I reply. Very sophisticated. I’ll be going to law school at William and Mary in the fall, so I suppose it’s time to start raising the bar. The Lambda hands me fine china—a green Solo cup—and I take a sip. The wine is dry and not very good. The bass from outside thrums, causing my lightheadedness to build into a migraine. I curse myself for getting older. Conveniently, Simer laments about having to use the bathroom. Because one person has to pee, suddenly everyone does. I begin down the hall, pushing open random doors. Eventually one gives way to a bathroom. Over one of the urinals is a poster of a blond woman taking off her glasses and looking down at, well, you know. I snap a photo to laugh at later. The girls and I each take turns in the stall as my head pulses. Hannah watches me wince each time the door slams shut and suggests we go somewhere quiet. We’ve been best friends long enough for her to know I get these chronically. We find a set of rocking chairs outside on the lawn and sit. We people-watch in silence until we finish what’s inside our fine china. These weeks will be filled with many lasts. The last strawberry-acai refresher from the Tate Student Center Starbucks, the last time braving the million-dollar staircase on North Campus, my last sorority date night, last bar crawl, and, today, my last fraternity darty. I’m on the precipice of a new chapter. I don’t know what it has in store for me. I’ve been through a pandemic, and AI is coming. What I do know is I’ll never be this young again, surrounded by all the people I love at once. I glance down at my phone to check my boyfriend’s location. I’d texted him from the bathroom that I was ready to be picked up; he’s now two minutes away. “Do any of y’all need a ride downtown or back to the apartment?” I ask. Hannah turns to me, her smile flickering briefly. “I think we’re going to stay a bit longer.” —Gabrielle Gruszynski, UGA ’26 Professional Bull Riders, based in Fort Worth, Texas, has brought the classic cowboy sport to the world. Events are viewed in more than seventy countries. Keyshawn Whitehorse, a proud rider from the Navajo Nation in southeast Utah and the 2018 PBR Rookie of the Year, told Esquire’s Josh Rosenberg about taking on bull Kinky Brahmer at a PBR event in Billings. I take a good look at the animal. Really stare it in the eye. There’s a lot you can glean before a ride. My bull tonight is big for his age. It takes a little while for a young bull to get seasoned. So I can already tell that Brahmer is dangerous. If I have my foot in the wrong spot, he’ll crush it before the gates even open. The music cuts through my focus. “If you want a cowboy on a white horse ... hold on tight, girl.” My walk-on song: “White Horse” by Chris Stapleton. I didn’t choose it—the tour did, probably because of my name—but I guess you could say it’s been good to me. I’ve qualified for the World Finals eight times. But tonight, Brahmer won’t make reaching a ninth easy. I’m already hot when I enter the chute. Most people probably wouldn’t expect that from just an eight-second bull ride. But when the adrenaline kicks in, you build up a good bit of sweat. I say my prayers, hop on, the gate opens, and he kicks immediately. You’re just reacting. A wrong move made with intention will put you in a better place than a right move made in hesitation. I dig my knees into his side to check him, but he takes a bigger leap than I expect. I’m rocked to the back. And once you’re rocked to the back, you’re in the one area you want to stay away from. They call it the bull’s territory. There’s nothing in the world like it. Some bulls are just rock-solid. All muscle. When you’re a split second late you get bucked off—hard. I’m thrown after five seconds, and he leaves me behind. Tonight, the bull wins. I’m chasing a rematch now. When you respect the animal and conquer the fear, a ride makes you feel on top of the world. Cassy, a sixty-one-year-old platinum-blond dancer in black garters, wraps matches around her nipples as Taylor Swift’s “Style” plays. Within seconds, her areolas are illuminated by flames. She sways her hips on the stage behind the oval bar. “Fire tits!” shouts a dude young enough to be her grandson. A middle-aged dentist from out of town watches the flames peter out. She’s nursing a canned cocktail, smiling so wide her eyes crinkle beneath her black bob. Cassy gyrates to her side of the bar. The stripper places a mini basketball hoop in front of her crotch. The dentist balls up a dollar bill. Swish! “She looks like my mom!” the dentist shouts, then throws back her head, laughing. Her sour mood has evaporated. So has the memory of the heated argument with her husband that occurred just before. She doesn’t know where he is and doesn’t care. After one more dance, Cassy gathers the singles scattered across the stage, tosses them into a bucket, and lowers herself to the floor. Lexi, heavily pregnant, follows Cassy. Lizzo’s “Truth Hurts” pumps through the speakers as she emerges in a scarlet fishnet bra-and-pantie set that matches her curly red hair. Her Lucite heels are much shorter than other dancers’ stilettos—but that’s her only concession to pregnancy. She lowers her bra, grabs her left boob, and shimmies with the grace of a woman without a fetus inside of her. A married couple eye Lexi from their barstools. They’re celebrating their anniversary. “We’re white-trash people,” says the mid-thirties husband in hipster glasses. “We thought, Let’s have a beer and look at boobs.” His wife, in her early twenties with blond-streaked hair, feels like she can be herself here. “I don’t have to flash a bunch of money and look a certain way,” she says. Her mom was a stripper, her dad a strip-club bouncer. He sat at these same stools and watched some of the same strippers, like sixty-­nine-year-old Blondie, who follows Lexi. “My dad told me stories about her crushing beer cans with her tits,” she says. —Hallie Lieberman Leaving for the casino by day is what I call “going to church.” It’s my personal none-of-your-business assertion of an important ritual journey: a cold bottle of water, a drive through the urban wreckage at the fringe of a city, over hill and dale, betwixt river and mountain, to the business of a casino. It’s a spare pilgrimage built for a Friday afternoon, when the casino plays the part of a cathedral beckoning from a distance. Churches have only a couple of appointed entrances, and casinos too have just three main doorways max. Inside? No real windows in either, at least none you can see through clearly. Churches filter and recolor the intrusion of the outside world through occluded glass so it falls on the pews like dewy light from above. Casinos block the outside world with a wall of sound and scorching colors cast from row upon row of slot machines, built with sixty-five-inch HD televisions tilted on their ends. I like to tell myself both places are like that by design, to help with focus and self-reflection. So, yeah, on this day, like a good American, I go to church. And now I’m riding midnight again, after the usual exercises—hours in the poker room, one not-so-lucky half hour at the roulette wheel, another long stab at a slot machine, where I hit a single jackpot that righted all the wrongs that came to me as the night fell. All of it has left me, twenty minutes from midnight, more or less even for the day. When I sit down at another fifty-dollar blackjack table, I can finally see the energy of the place from my seat. The joint skews young and thumping, mad-jammed with the aforementioned light and sound, overpopulated by payday pilgrims of the dice, with throngs of date-night couples decked in their Friday best, cleavage and biceps at the ready, and roving herds of bachelor and bachelorette parties looking to stampede the night in search of its limits. There are five people sitting at the felt curl of the table when I finally take the last seat on the left, while the dealer shuffles. “How’s this been going?” I quietly ask the beefy dude to my right, a thirty-ish guy wearing an anachronistic black blazer over a bejeweled wifebeater with the exclamation “Caliente” pointed in sequins. He doesn’t answer, and the five measly chips stacked on the felt at his gut line pretty much tell the story. A bad run. He’s caught in a head shake/death stare at the apparition of luck in the space in front of him. In fact, I can see then that everyone at the table looks a bit shaggy and unnerved by their last round of cards. Not one word from any of them. There it was. That was the sign. I see that now. End of day, when midnight falls. I should have stood right up and vamoosed. Should have been happy to be even, rather than flummoxed by the fact and willing to take more. But I was night-blind at that point, locked in on the sense that I might be there to reset the luck—to bring the luck, even. You know the story from here. Like a robot of faith I drop two chips onto the table seven times, I double up twice, and the house hits blackjack twice in a row. I then double my original bet, we push on twenty twice, before I bust on thirteen. Eight straight hands. Just like that, I have tilted and tithed nine hundred bucks to the Indian nation that runs the joint. When I finally unlock my own death stare at luck from the space before me, I look around the table and register. Beefy Caliente is long gone, the others too. I stand up to go as the table repopulates with young women in matching tiaras, each gripping tall, curved cocktail boots. They smell like bananas. I leave. At the exit I step into the night and fill my lungs with cool desert air. The outside world rushes in to comfort me. I breathe more. The valet greets me, like a pastor after the service to shake hands with my folded-up tip. “Have a good morning, sir,” he says, even out there with the starlit envelope of night still spread above us. Car keys in hand, I breathe some more until I can see he’s right. This too is a new day. —Tom Chiarella My Weekend Getaway with the Proud Boys Wait, Tulsi Gabbard Was a Weird Religious Puppet? Title: The founder of China's Amazon wants his 700,000 blue-collar workers to be 'white-collarized' in time for AI
URL: https://www.businessinsider.com/jd-com-founder-richard-liu-ai-blue-collar-workers-robots-2026-6#article
Time Published: 2026-06-23T08:41:34Z
Full Content:
The founder of China's Amazon equivalent said his 700,000 blue-collar delivery workers will become office workers in the years to come. Richard Liu, the billionaire founder of China's e-commerce behemoth JD.com, said the company has several "Nirvana Plans" underway to "white-collarize" its 700,000 blue-collar workers, including delivery riders. Speaking at the Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation CEO forum on Sunday, Liu said that in the age of AI, workers won't be needed for deliveries. Still, they will be needed to repair or troubleshoot the robots doing the manual labor. So instead of working in the field, the workers will be sitting in offices, and won't have to work nearly as hard. "JD.com has therefore signed contracts with 120 schools across China to help these blue-collar workers retrain for new roles, including repairing and maintaining robots," Liu said. Liu added that he was advocating for an internationally recognized protocol for adopting AI and robots as social systems change. He said robots should not deprive people of the right to work. The executive also said in May that JD.com would not fire any employee whose job was being replaced by robots; instead, they would be retrained and reassigned elsewhere. Deliveries via robots are already commonplace in China, including drones used to deliver food packages. For instance, food delivery giant Meituan said in 2024 that its drones could be deployed to deliver packages to hikers on the Great Wall of China. Take a smarter break in your day - and see how far you get. Liu's comments come as e-commerce giants around the world are ramping up their automation efforts to boost productivity. Amazon has a fleet of more than 750,000 robots working in its fulfillment centers. Other companies have rolled out fleets of delivery robots on city streets. DoorDash launched bright red Dot robots in Phoenix last September, which can travel on footpaths and carry up to 30 pounds of cargo. Companies like Robot.com and Starship are targeting deliveries on college campuses. Beyond gig work, automation and AI are the biggest buzzwords tied to corporate layoffs this year. Companies like Snap, Wix, and Cisco cited AI as a factor in their recent retrenchments. Jump to Every time publishes a story, you’ll get an alert straight to your inbox! Title: Lazada cuts 5% of South-east Asia staff, including in S'pore
URL: https://www.straitstimes.com/business/lazada-cuts-5-of-south-east-asia-workforce-including-roles-in-singapore
Time Published: 2026-06-23T06:13:22Z
Full Content:
Choose edition Search singapore asia world opinion life business sport Visual Podcasts STClassifieds Paid press releases Advertise with us FAQs Contact us Sign up now: Get ST's newsletters delivered to your inbox A Lazada spokesperson said on June 23 that the regional e-commerce company is “reviewing selected roles across South-east Asia”. ST PHOTO: SHINTARO TAY Sharon Salim Published Jun 23, 2026, 02:13 PM Updated Jun 23, 2026, 08:36 PM SINGAPORE - Regional e-commerce company Lazada has retrenched 5 per cent of its workforce in South-east Asia, including an undisclosed number of employees in Singapore, according to a source familiar with the matter. When contacted, a Lazada spokesperson said on June 23 that the company is “reviewing selected roles across South-east Asia”. “Where roles are affected, employees will be engaged directly and with care, respect and dignity,” the spokesperson told The Straits Times. “We are committed to supporting affected employees through the transition with relevant guidance and support, in line with applicable requirements.” This comes after Lazada’s e-commerce rival Shopee axed hundreds of developer jobs globally earlier in June. In early May, Amazon also announced job cuts in Singapore as the e-commerce giant shifted its focus from local grocery fulfilment to international product shipments. Derrick Teo, chief executive of manpower consultancy firm Elitez Group, said the e-commerce industry is still growing, but the period of rapid hiring, heavy subsidies and growth at all costs has ended. “Platforms are expected to process more transactions while controlling costs and improving profitability. Technology and artificial intelligence are helping companies operate more efficiently, meaning business growth may no longer produce the same level of headcount growth seen during the (Covid-19) pandemic,” he said. Online chatter surfaced about two weeks ago when a Reddit user commented about Lazada’s impending job cuts in a forum that discusses recent layoffs and workplace culture in Singapore. ST understands that affected Lazada employees were notified on June 23, with its human resources team engaging them directly. Its staff in Singapore are based in the Lazada One office in Bras Basah Road. Lazada said it is working closely with the Food, Drinks and Allied Workers Union (FDAWU) to ensure support is rendered for affected employees and for the process to be “handled responsibly”. FDAWU general-secretary Sankaradass S. Chami said Lazada had informed the union in advance about the job cuts. “This advance notification reflects the positive labour-management relationship and trust built over time,” he said, adding that Lazada will provide one year of union membership and training grant support for affected union members. Lazada previously laid off an undisclosed number of staff in January 2024 without consulting the FDAWU, under which it is unionised. The union had expressed its disappointment then, and the matter was escalated to the Ministry of Manpower (MOM). The Taskforce for Responsible Retrenchment and Employment Facilitation also noted that Lazada had notified the task force about its restructuring exercise, which is part of a larger organisational realignment. “Lazada is committed to providing a retrenchment package aligned with the Tripartite Advisory on Managing Excess Manpower and Responsible Retrenchment,” said a spokesperson for the task force, which comprises MOM, Workforce Singapore, National Trades Union Congress and NTUC’s e2i (Employment and Employability Institute). In the 2024 job cuts, the FDAWU negotiated improved retrenchment benefits for the laid-off Lazada employees, who had initially been offered two weeks’ salary for each year of service – a figure below industry norms. MOM facilitated the discussions between the union and Lazada. Affected employees were notified months later that some would receive an extra two weeks’ pay per year of service, while others would get training support of $1,200. A round of layoffs had also been conducted in October 2023. Lazada’s 2024 job cuts turned the spotlight on the company, increasing speculation about a potential initial public offering for its parent business unit that year. It came amid growing competition in South-east Asia’s e-commerce landscape. Postings on professional networking platform LinkedIn suggest that Lazada has about 760 employees in Singapore. A June 23 check by ST on job openings on the platform shows at least 10 roles in Bangkok, Manila and the Philippines. A job opening for Singapore was posted a month ago. On the Lazada website, however, no job openings can be found. Founded in 2012, Lazada, which became a subsidiary of Chinese tech giant Alibaba Group Holding in 2016, has a presence in Singapore, Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines, Thailand and Vietnam. While Lazada remains the second-largest e-commerce player in Singapore, just behind Shopee, its estimated market share in South-east Asia fell in 2025 as its rivals grew faster, said Teo. “This stronger competition may have pushed Lazada to accelerate its restructuring and operate with a leaner workforce, which contributed to the retrenchment exercise. However, this remains an industry-based interpretation as Lazada officially attributed the cuts to business needs and organisational efficiency,” he added. Lazada recently marked its 14th anniversary with a birthday sale, its first major regional campaign for 2026. The platform’s use of AI features has become more apparent as they are integrated into product pages. Additional reporting by Vihanya Rakshika and Emerald Lo Sharon Salim is a business correspondent at The Straits Times, with a focus on jobs, workplace culture and trends. Title: Bouygues Telecom Enhances Quality-of-Experience for Millions of French Broadband Subscribers with AI-Driven Expresse from AXON Networks
URL: https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/bouygues-telecom-enhances-quality-of-experience-for-millions-of-french-broadband-subscribers-with-ai-driven-expresse-from-axon-networks-302806762.html
Time Published: 2026-06-23T06:00:00Z
Full Content:
Searching for your content... In-Language News Contact Us 888-776-0942 from 8 AM - 10 PM ET Jun 23, 2026, 02:00 ET Share this article Leading French service provider upgrades broadband service assurance capabilities with advanced AI solution delivering enhanced network visibility and a superior customer experience MEUDON LA FORÊT, France and IRVINE, Calif., June 23, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Bouygues Telecom, a leading France-based provider of mobile, internet, and IPTV services, and AXON Networks, a global leader in intelligent autonomous network and connectivity assurance platforms, today announced that Bouygues Telecom is expanding their long-term deployment of the Expresse Access Network Assurance solution, part of the AXON Maestro platform, to support both DSL and PON environments. As part of this evolution, Bouygues Telecom is replacing its legacy SNMP based monitoring service assurance platform with the Expresse solution, featuring an architecture focused on AI analysis, telemetry and modern GUI for efficient customer support. This strategic shift will enable significantly deeper, real-time visibility across complex, multi-vendor network environments. By leveraging the AI-driven analytics and streaming telemetry of Expresse, Bouygues Telecom will enhance the quality-of-experience for approximately six million fixed broadband subscribers nationwide, while improving the management of field operations and accelerating issue resolution as Bouygues moves toward autonomous networking. The deployment reflects a broader transition toward next-generation service assurance, replacing legacy tools with AXON Network's AI-native, more scalable and future-ready approach that leverages deep domain expertise built from the over 97 million subscriber lines globally currently leveraging their solutions. "As our network evolves toward greater scale and complexity, it has been essential to move beyond the limitations of traditional SNMP-based monitoring," said Jean-Paul Arzel, Chief Technical Officer of Bouygues Telecom. "Expresse from AXON Networks will provide the depth of visibility and multi-vendor telemetry capabilities we were looking for, enabling a more proactive and automated approach to service assurance. This path of evolution will play a key role in improving both operational efficiency and customer experience." "Service providers are increasingly recognizing the need to modernize legacy assurance environments that were not designed for today's fiber-centric, multi-vendor networks," said Martin Manniche, CEO and founder of AXON Networks. "Bouygues Telecom's expansion of Expresse reflects a broader industry shift toward real-time telemetry and AI-driven operations that optimize performance, autonomous networks, and connectivity experience. We are proud to support them in this transformation." About Expresse Expresse features a sophisticated diagnostics engine leveraging artificial intelligence, machine learning, and modern streaming telemetry, all hallmarks of the AXON Maestro platform, to transform how network performance is monitored and managed. Unlike traditional SNMP-based systems—which provide limited, periodic snapshots of network health—Expresse harnesses real-time, high-frequency telemetry data across multi-vendor environments, delivering granular and continuous visibility into network and service performance. Key capabilities include: Expresse and its AI-driven analytics combine industry-leading telemetry with over a decade of domain expertise drawn from the network and service experience of many of the world's largest broadband operators to provide unrivalled insights into network optimization and performance improvement. Service providers using Expresse have reported: About Bouygues Telecom A subsidiary of the Bouygues group, Bouygues Telecom is a global French telecommunications and digital services operator. In 2025, the company achieved a turnover of 8.1 billion euros, employs 10,700 people, and has 501 stores in France. Founded in 1994, Bouygues Telecom is committed to providing its individual customers, businesses, and public administrations with secure, innovative, and high-quality Fixed and Mobile communication services, as well as very high-speed internet services, by constantly developing its network and user experience. 27.1 million Mobile customers and 5.4 million Fixed customers trust Bouygues Telecom, the number 1 operator for WiFi and mobile internet connections according to nPerf in 2025, and number 1 for Mobile in dense areas according to ARCEP, in 2025. Its 4G network now covers 99% of the population, and its 5G network covers more than 19,900 municipalities and over 86% of the population. Bouygues Telecom's Enterprise division supports and helps grow a community of over 120,000 clients, including four out of five major CAC 40 accounts, by generalizing Very High-Speed Broadband in France and facilitating the adoption of new uses such as unified communications, smart networks, and corporate mobility services, and by supporting the transformation of their digital infrastructures. Committed to reducing its carbon emissions, Bouygues Telecom aims to achieve -29.4% for scopes 1 and 2 and -17.5% for scope 3 by 2027, objectives approved by the Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi). #OnEstFaitPourEtreEnsemble To follow Bouygues Telecom news: corporate.bouyguestelecom.fr, on X: @ByTel_Corporate About AXON Networks AXON Networks is a global leader in intelligent autonomous network and connectivity assurance platforms, known for its award-winning Digital Twin technology that enables real-time, predictive, and self-optimizing operations and extraordinary connectivity experiences. Its AXON Maestro platform and solutions are used by service providers and enterprises worldwide to unlock the full potential of advanced networks, transforming them into agile AI connectivity enablers with hyperscaler-like network operations. AXON Network's platforms are relied upon by over 30 leading operators worldwide to serve a rapidly increasing base of over 97 million managed users and provide unrivalled AN-4 focused network autonomy, orchestration, security, and insights to bring the best AI connected experiences to everyone, everywhere.
